ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific oolong milk tea with less sugar variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM covers tea-based beverages with milk; no oolong-specific less-sugar variant code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for oolong milk tea beverage variant. Null retained to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: blended with milk or non-dairy creamer

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of oolong milk tea (less sugar, no topping) is approximately 250 ml, equivalent to a typical takeaway cup size served at Singaporean bubble tea or kopitiam outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Oolong Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for oolong milk tea (less sugar, no topping), cr_id D08010075. All per-100g values preserved from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Milk Tea and Tea-Based Beverages with Sugar — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for oolong milk tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on: (1) plain oolong tea has negligible GI; (2) milk tea with reduced sugar analogues in the Sydney GI database cluster in the 40–55 range; (3) the 'less sugar' formulation reduces the sucrose contribution relative to standard milk tea (GI ~55–65). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (6.56 g/100g × 250 g serving = 16.4 g) / 100 = 7.4. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar and non-dairy creamer despite low sodium
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low at 19 mg/100 g, the added sugar (4.9 g/100 g) and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer can contribute to weight gain and endothelial dysfunction, which are secondary drivers of elevated blood pressure; individuals with hypertension should limit sweetened milk tea and prefer unsweetened oolong tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (SSB classification) and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer
type2_diabetes.guidance: Even at 'less sugar', this beverage contains 4.9 g of sugar per 100 g and qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit SSBs as they cause rapid postprandial glucose excursions, and the saturated fat from non-dairy creamer further impairs insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from non-dairy creamer and added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat content of 3.37 g/100 g, predominantly saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) from palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer, combined with added sugar, raises LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose unsweetened oolong tea with low-fat dairy milk as a safer alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oolong milk tea (less sugar, no topping) is a popular Singaporean tea-shop and bubble-tea-outlet beverage made by brewing oolong tea and blending it with non-dairy creamer or milk, sweetened at a reduced sugar level compared to the standard formulation, and served without any additional toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. Oolong tea is a partially oxidised Chinese tea that sits between green and black tea in flavour profile, offering a smooth, slightly floral, and mildly roasted taste. The addition of creamer gives the drink a milky, rounded mouthfeel. At the 'less sugar' customisation level, the sweetness is noticeably reduced but residual sugar remains. Per 100 g, the beverage provides 57 kcal, 0.15 g protein, 3.37 g fat (predominantly saturated from non-dairy creamer), 6.56 g carbohydrate, 4.9 g sugar, 1.78 g dietary fibre, and 19 mg sodium. Despite the low sodium content, the beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its added sugar content, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The oolong tea base contains polyphenols with antioxidant properties, but these benefits are partially offset by the pro-inflammatory saturated fat from palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer. This beverage is commonly consumed as a breakfast or afternoon drink in Singapore and is widely available at bubble tea chains and kopitiams.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010075) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g/100 g based on the fat profile typical of palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer as documented in HPB and USDA reference data for similar milk tea preparations; the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this specific beverage variant. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 45, low category) derived from University of Sydney GI Database analogues for reduced-sugar milk tea beverages; a direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available in any approved source. 4. Dietary fibre at 1.78 g/100 g is noted as unusually high for a liquid tea beverage and is preserved as reported by HPB; this may reflect soluble fibre contributions from oolong tea polyphenols or a formulation-specific ingredient. 5. The 'less sugar' designation corresponds to approximately 70% of standard sugar level at most Singaporean bubble tea outlets. 6. Non-dairy creamer used in this beverage may contain trans fats depending on the brand; trans_fat_g is set to -1 as the HPB record does not specify.
